topic: Understanding mouse-driven interactivity in p5.js, specifically the distinction between continuous state polling and discrete event-driven functions.

concepts.name: State Polling (mouseIsPressed)
concepts.explanation: Checking a boolean variable inside the draw loop to see if the mouse button is currently being held down. It's a continuous check that happens every frame.
concepts.why_it_matters: Essential for actions that need to happen continuously while a button is held, like drawing a line as you move the mouse.

concepts.name: Event-Driven Functions (mouseClicked, mousePressed, etc.)
concepts.explanation: Functions that are called by the system only once when a specific action occurs (like clicking or releasing). They live outside the draw loop.
concepts.why_it_matters: Used for actions that should only trigger once per interaction, like spawning an object or changing a setting, to avoid the code running every single frame.

concepts.name: The Mouse Click Lifecycle
concepts.explanation: A 'click' is a sequence of events: the mouse must be over an element, the button must be pressed down, and then released while still over that same element.
concepts.why_it_matters: Helps students understand why certain functions (like mouseClicked) might not trigger if the user moves the mouse too much during a click.

code_examples.demonstrates: Continuous drawing: as long as the mouse is held, a line is drawn from the origin to the current mouse position every frame.
code_examples.common_issue: The line will appear as a continuous stroke because it's being updated every single frame in the draw loop.

code_examples.demonstrates: Discrete event: a line is drawn only once at the exact moment the mouse button is released after being pressed.
code_examples.common_issue: If you try to use this for continuous drawing, nothing will happen because it only triggers once per click.

code_examples.demonstrates: Triggering an action specifically when the user lets go of the mouse button.
Drawing a shape in the center of the screen only when the user double-clicks.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: Unexpected token error / Code doesn't run
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Typing the function name incorrectly (e.g., 'mouseClicked' with a lowercase 'd' instead of the required syntax, or misspelling keywords).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Ensure function names match p5.js requirements exactly (e.g., `mouseClicked`).

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The drawing is too 'heavy' or continuous when I only wanted one thing to happen
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Using `if (mouseIsPressed)` inside the `draw()` loop instead of using an event function.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Move the logic into a discrete event function like `mouseClicked()` outside of the draw loop.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The event doesn't trigger even though I clicked
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The mouse was pressed on one element but released outside of it (the 'boundary' was broken).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Understand that `mouseClicked` requires the press and release to happen within the same boundary.

key_moments.description: The instructor highlights the 'problem' of continuous execution in the draw loop, creating productive struggle by showing how `mouseIsPressed` behaves differently than an event.
The instructor explains the 'direction' of logic: `draw()` is continuous/polling, while events happen in a different temporal dimension (only when triggered).
The instructor makes a typo and shows the error message, modeling how to recognize and fix syntax errors in real-time.

prerequisites: Basic p5.js setup (setup and draw functions)
Basic JavaScript syntax and function declarations
leads_to: Complex UI/UX design in creative coding
student_questions: Why is my line drawing a huge mess instead of just one single line?
How do I make something happen only once when I click?
What is the difference between mouseIsPressed and mouseClicked?
Why isn't my click working even though I clicked the button?
How do I detect a double click?
Why does my code only run when I click on the canvas first?
How do I draw something specifically when I let go of the mouse?
What's the difference between a state and an event?
My code says 'unexpected token', what did I do wrong?
How can I make my art react to the mouse?
